title: practical english usage - Part 138
content:
We   do not use personal pronouns to repeat the meaning of relative pronouns 6  personal       and   relative      pronouns:      one   or  the   other,    not   both For it as a preparatory subject or object, ►    268-269.

He's not a bad bloke, Jeff. Its a horrible place, London.

There are exceptions in very informal speech (►  299).

The situation is terrible, (     It is temble-the-situation.) not The budget forecast was reasonably accurate, (                       forwist it was Section14 Pronouns grammar •  174   personal pronouns: advanced points not It’s for him  to decide, (     ITsfor-he^e-declde/) in this case.

subject of a following infinitive or clause. Normally an object form is used Sometimes a pronoun can be seen as the object of a verb or preposition, but the 4 mixed     subject      and   object:    Its  for    him     to   decide.

He was the person / the one who told the police.

We can avoid being too formal or too informal by using a different structure.

It is I who need your help.  It was he who told the police.

subject form + who (very formal) It’s me that needs your help. it was him  that told the police.

object form + that (very informal) two possibilities: When a relative clause comes after an expression like It is/was me/1, there are 3 It  is!was     me    that    ...// who     ...

Nobody but me knew the answer.

Everybody except him  can come, (     Everybody except he can comer) (►  413,  456).

But (meaning ’ except’ ) and except are normally followed by object forms My sister’s nearly as tall as I am. I can run faster than she can.

In a more formal style, subject forms are used, usually followed by verbs.

My sister’s nearly as tall as me. I can run faster than her.

After as and than, object forms are generally used in an informal style.

2 as,   than,     but    and   except        + me    or  I formal speech or writing.

however, restricted to an informal style. They  are not considered correct in coordinated subjects in Jane Austen’ s novels, written around 1800.) They  are, common in educated speech for centuries. (There are examples of me in These structures are often condemned as ’ incorrect’ , but they have been Us women understand these things better than you men.

Us is sometimes used as a subject together with a noun.

Elizabeth, wife of the future King George VI, to King Edward VIII).

I often think of the old days and how you helped Bertie and I. (letter from Thanks, Andrew - Feb. 23rd is good for both Jack and I.

Really nice picture of Josh and I taken at the weekend by my friend Joe.

Between you and I, I think his marriage is in trouble.

Me and Clio will be coming to see you and Mum  on Sunday.

Jack and them are going skiing this weekend.

examples from speakers of standard British English: writing. And I is often used informally in coordinated objects. 'Dad said I could go out.' 'No, I didn't.’ (More polite than He said I could to refer to people who  are present.

It is considered polite to use names or noun phrases, rather than he, she or they, 10 politeness not (    He~She-who-leat*es Iasi ...) or The person who leaves last should lock the door,  Whoever leaves last...

But this is very unusual in modern English.

He who hesitates is sometimes lost.

literature.

The  structure he/she who ... (meaning 'the person who  ...') is found in older 9 he/she       who     ...

For the older English forms thee and thou, ►    318.10.

Hi, everybody. How're you all doing? What are you all's plans for is also a possessive you all's (pronounced y'all's).

y'all), used instead of you when people wish to sound friendly or intimate; there In southern US speech there is a second person plural form you all (pronounced Hi, you guys. Listen to this.
